title: Quiz: Resource Theories Framework
description: Test your understanding of quantum resource theories and their mathematical structure

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a required component of a quantum resource theory?

options.text: Free states - states that can be prepared at no cost
Free operations - operations implementable without consuming resources
A unique maximally resourceful state
Resource states - states outside the free set

questions.explanation: A resource theory is defined by free states, free operations, and resource states (the complement of free states). While many resource theories have a 'golden unit' maximally resourceful state, this is not a required component of the general framework.

questions.question: A function M(ρ) is a valid resource monotone if it satisfies which properties?

options.text: M(ρ) = 0 for all states and M never increases
M(ρ) = 0 iff ρ is free, and M(E(ρ)) ≤ M(ρ) for free operations E
M(ρ) is always positive and additive under tensor products
M(ρ) equals the trace distance to the nearest free state

questions.explanation: A resource monotone must satisfy faithfulness (M(ρ) = 0 iff ρ is free) and monotonicity under free operations (M(E(ρ)) ≤ M(ρ)). These ensure the monotone correctly identifies and orders resources.

questions.question: In the resource theory of entanglement, what are the free states?

options.text: Pure states
Maximally entangled states
Separable states
Thermal states

questions.explanation: In entanglement theory, separable states (states of the form ρ = Σᵢ pᵢ ρᵢᴬ ⊗ ρᵢᴮ) are the free states. These can be prepared using only LOCC (Local Operations and Classical Communication), which are the free operations.

questions.question: The relative entropy of resource R(ρ) = min_{σ∈F} D(ρ||σ) has which key property?

options.text: It is always negative for resource states
It equals zero only for maximally resourceful states
It satisfies monotonicity under all quantum channels
It is non-negative and equals zero iff ρ is free

questions.explanation: The relative entropy of resource inherits non-negativity from the relative entropy (D(ρ||σ) ≥ 0) and equals zero when ρ is in the free set F (since then the minimum over σ∈F includes ρ itself, giving D(ρ||ρ) = 0).

questions.question: If M(ρ) ≥ M(σ) for all resource monotones M, then ρ can always be converted to σ using free operations.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. Monotone ordering is necessary but not always sufficient for state conversion. Some transformations are impossible even when all known monotones allow them. This is related to the existence of infinitely many monotones and phenomena like catalysis.

questions.question: What does the 'closure of free states' axiom require?

options.text: The set of free states must be topologically closed
Free operations acting on free states produce free states
All states can eventually become free
Free states form a finite set

questions.explanation: The closure axiom states that free operations cannot create resources from free states: if σ∈F and E is a free operation, then E(σ)∈F. This ensures the consistency of the resource theory framework.

questions.question: In asymptotic resource manipulation, the distillation rate R_d(ρ) represents:

options.text: The rate at which resources decay over time
The maximum rate of extracting pure resource units from many copies of ρ
The probability of successful resource measurement
The cost of preparing the state ρ

questions.explanation: The distillation rate R_d(ρ) is the maximum number of pure 'golden unit' resource states that can be extracted per copy of ρ in the asymptotic limit of many copies, using only free operations.

questions.question: Which statements about resource theories are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Convex monotones satisfy M(Σᵢ pᵢρᵢ) ≤ Σᵢ pᵢM(ρᵢ)
The tensor product of free states is always free
All resource theories have reversible asymptotic transformations
Resource witnesses can detect some but not all resource states

questions.explanation: (a) Correct: This is the definition of convexity for monotones. (b) Correct: This is one of the axioms of resource theories. (c) Incorrect: Many resource theories exhibit irreversibility where distillation and dilution rates differ. (d) Correct: A single witness has a limited detection range; different witnesses may be needed for different states.
